scenario: Vanta Bank builds a list of 3 nested pair lists on a service task input mapping in Camunda 8.8 across 13000 transactions per day. The FEEL literal must hold each pair as its own list value while still presenting a single outer list to the downstream worker for further filtering.
question: Which property of FEEL list literals do nested lists such as the literal containing 1, 2 and 3, 4 and 5, 6 illustrate?

options.text: List literals require all elements to share the same scalar type, so a literal that holds inner list values is rejected at evaluation time as a heterogeneous container
List literals allow elements of any type, including other list literals, so an outer literal can embed inner list values without flattening, as documented in the list expressions reference
List literals embed inner lists only when each inner list is wrapped inside a context literal first, with the engine flattening any bare inner list at the outer literal boundary
List literals embed inner lists only when the count of inner lists equals the count of outer elements, with the engine zipping the two structures together during evaluation

a.text: Incorrect. The list expressions reference states that elements can be of any type, so a list literal is allowed to mix scalar and list elements in the same value.

b.text: Correct. The Literal entry of the list expressions reference states that the elements can be of any type and gives a worked example with three nested inner pair lists embedded in an outer list literal.

c.text: Incorrect. Inner lists do not need to be wrapped in a context literal; the documented example embeds inner lists directly without any wrapping value.

d.text: Incorrect. The reference does not constrain the count of inner lists to match the count of outer elements; nested lists embed freely without any zip rule.

explanation: FEEL list literals allow elements of any type, including other list literals, so an outer literal can carry nested inner list values without any flattening or wrapping requirement.
